seo.title: Sing Buri Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Sing Buri Province: the heroic Bang Rachan village resistance monument, the revered reclining Buddha at Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan, traditional Thai sweets from Inburi district, and tranquil rice-plain landscapes. One of Thailand's smallest provinces. Area: 822 km², population: 211,000.

overview: Sing Buri is one of Thailand's smallest provinces by land area, yet it carries an outsized weight of historical memory and cultural pride within the Central Plains. Tucked between Ang Thong to the south, Lop Buri to the east, and Chai Nat to the west, approximately 142 km north of Bangkok, the province is defined by two things above all else: a story of extraordinary human courage and an atmosphere of deep, unhurried agricultural calm. The name 'Sing Buri' is believed to derive from the Sanskrit 'Simhapura' — City of the Lion — reflecting the region's ancient Indianised heritage. The province's most celebrated landmark is the Bang Rachan Heroes Monument, which commemorates a group of villagers from the Bang Rachan settlement who mounted a prolonged armed resistance against the Burmese army during the siege of Ayutthaya in 1765. For five months, these ordinary farmers and villagers — armed with improvised weapons and fuelled by devotion to their community and their faith — repulsed repeated Burmese assaults before ultimately being overwhelmed. Their story became a defining legend of Thai national identity: proof that courage and determination can outlast superior force. Today the monument and the surrounding village sites attract Thai pilgrims and students learning the nation's history. Beyond this martial legacy, Sing Buri is also home to Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan, whose colossal and deeply revered reclining Buddha draws merit-making pilgrims from across the Central Plains. The district of Inburi is celebrated throughout Thailand as a centre of traditional sweet-making, its artisan confectioners producing sugar-palm and coconut-based desserts using recipes unchanged for generations. The province's character is fundamentally agricultural and rural: its flat alluvial plains, canal networks, and modest market towns move at a pace set by the rice cycle and the Buddhist calendar rather than by tourism.
geography: Sing Buri covers approximately 822 km² of flat alluvial lowland in the heart of the Central Plains — making it one of the four or five smallest provinces in the country by land area. The terrain is almost entirely level, formed by centuries of deposition from the Chao Phraya river system, and rarely rises more than a few metres above sea level. The Pa Sak River flows through the eastern portion of the province before joining the Chao Phraya system, and a network of irrigation canals cross-crosses the agricultural interior, channelling water into the paddy fields and orchards that form the economic foundation of the province. There are no significant hills, mountains, or forests; the landscape is an open plain of rice paddies, cassava fields, and sugar-palm groves, interspersed with temple compounds and traditional village communities. The provincial capital sits on a bend of the Chao Phraya River in the west of the province. Sing Buri borders Nakhon Sawan and Uthai Thani to the north, Lop Buri to the east, Ang Thong to the south, and Suphan Buri and Chai Nat to the west.
highlights: Bang Rachan Heroes Monument — a deeply moving national monument commemorating the village defenders who held off the Burmese army for five months in 1765 before the fall of Ayutthaya; a defining story of Thai courage and the province's most powerful historical landmark

Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan — home to one of the most highly revered reclining Buddha images in Central Thailand, drawing tens of thousands of Thai Buddhist pilgrims annually for merit-making and devotional visits

Inburi traditional sweet-making — the Inburi district is a nationally recognised centre of artisan Thai confectionery, producing sugar-palm sweets, coconut-based khanom, and traditional Central Plains desserts using recipes passed through generations

Agricultural rice-plain landscapes — the flat paddy-field panorama of Sing Buri's lowlands is among the most photogenic and authentic Central Plains rural environments in the country, at its most beautiful during the planting (June–July) and harvest (November–December) seasons

Pa Sak River corridor — the river and its associated canal network provide a tranquil backdrop for early-morning boat trips and bicycle rides through communities where traditional riverside life has changed little in generations

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Feb: The cool dry season is ideal for exploring Sing Buri's outdoor monuments and temples. Temperatures of 22–32°C with low humidity make the Bang Rachan monument, temple circuits, and Inburi sweet-market visits comfortable. The harvest season in November and December fills the paddy plains with golden rice.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Mar–May: The hot season across the flat Central Plains is intense, with temperatures regularly reaching 36–40°C by midday. Early morning visits to Wat Phra Non and Bang Rachan (before 10 AM) are manageable. Songkran in mid-April brings local water celebrations and temple merit-making.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Oct: The southwest monsoon transforms the province into a mosaic of brilliant green flooded paddies. Rainfall is moderate to heavy July through October but rarely disrupts visits to the major temples. The landscape is at its most agricultural and photogenic, and accommodation prices are at their lowest.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit between November and February for the most comfortable conditions and the golden-harvest landscape. A one-day circuit from Bangkok or Ayutthaya covering the Bang Rachan monument, Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan, and the Inburi sweet market is thoroughly feasible and deeply rewarding.

topAttractions.name: Bang Rachan Heroes Monument and Village
topAttractions.description: The most emotionally resonant historical site in Sing Buri Province and one of the most important patriotic landmarks in Thailand. In 1765, as the Burmese army advanced on Ayutthaya, the villagers of Bang Rachan — farmers, monks, and ordinary community members — organised themselves into a militia and repulsed repeated Burmese attacks for approximately five months. Despite sending urgent appeals for reinforcement and weapons from the Ayutthaya court, no help came. The village was eventually overwhelmed, and Ayutthaya fell the following year. But the story of the Bang Rachan defenders became a legend of national identity, preserved in chronicles, poetry, films, and school curricula for two and a half centuries. The modern monument complex includes a large open-air memorial with heroic statues of the village leaders, a museum documenting the historical events with artefacts and dioramas, and the reconstructed village site. Thai school groups, military cadets, and merit-making pilgrims visit throughout the year. The atmosphere is solemn, respectful, and deeply moving. A short distance from the monument is Wat Khao Din, associated with the village's final stand. The complex is free to enter and is signposted from Highway 32.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Sing Buri Province

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan
topAttractions.description: The spiritual centrepiece of Sing Buri Province and one of the most revered Buddhist temples in the Central Plains. The temple's principal sacred image is a large and deeply venerated reclining Buddha — Phra Non — whose serene posture and finely rendered features draw devotees from across Thailand for merit-making, healing prayers, and pilgrimage visits. The reclining posture symbolises the Buddha's passage into parinirvana and is associated in Thai Buddhist tradition with peace, liberation, and the release of suffering. The temple complex occupies a substantial riverside compound in the provincial capital area, with multiple viharn buildings, chedis of varying periods, and monks' residences set among shady trees. The atmosphere is one of active Buddhist devotion: incense smoke, offerings of flowers and gold leaf, and the quiet murmur of prayer are constant. The main viharn housing the reclining Buddha is elaborately decorated with traditional Thai mural paintings depicting scenes from the Jataka tales and the life of the Buddha. A visit here is an authentic encounter with Thai Buddhist pilgrimage culture, far removed from the tourist-facing temple experiences of Bangkok or Chiang Mai.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Sing Buri Province

topAttractions.name: Inburi District — Traditional Thai Sweet-Making
topAttractions.description: The district of Inburi, in the northern part of Sing Buri Province, holds a special place in Thai culinary culture as one of the country's most celebrated centres of traditional khanom (Thai confectionery) production. Artisan sweet-makers in Inburi's villages and market lanes have been producing sugar-palm and coconut-based sweets for generations, using recipes, tools, and techniques that have changed little over the centuries. The most characteristic products include khanom tan (sugar-palm sweets), khanom mor gaeng (egg-yolk custard tarts with coconut milk), and various coconut-based confections sold wrapped in banana leaf. The Inburi market is the best access point: stalls and small family workshops line the lanes of the old market town, and visitors can watch the production process — the slow stirring of coconut cream over charcoal, the careful moulding of sweets into traditional shapes — and purchase directly from the makers. Inburi khanom are considered premium quality and are brought back as gifts (of-wan, literally 'sweet things') by Thai visitors from across the region. The sweet-making tradition is an active, living craft, not a museum display.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Sing Buri Province

topAttractions.name: Wat Phra Phutthabat Yoi (Yoi Footprint Temple)
topAttractions.description: A hilltop temple in the northern part of the province enshrining a sacred Buddha footprint impression — one of several such revered sites in the Central Plains. The footprint (Roi Phra Phutthabat) is venerated as a direct physical trace of the Buddha's presence, and pilgrims ascend to the temple to make offerings, affix gold leaf, and pray for blessings. The elevated setting provides panoramic views across the flat paddy plains of Sing Buri — a rare opportunity to appreciate the province's characteristic landscape from above. The temple is active, with resident monks, and the climb to the main sanctuary is short but involves steps. A peaceful and atmospheric site that sees almost exclusively Thai devotees, giving it an entirely authentic pilgrimage character.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Sing Buri Province

topAttractions.name: Sing Buri National Museum
topAttractions.description: The provincial museum in the capital city, documenting Sing Buri's history from prehistoric settlement through the Dvaravati and Ayutthaya periods to the present. The collection includes archaeological finds from excavations in the province, Buddha images of different periods, traditional household and agricultural artefacts reflecting the rice-farming culture of the Central Plains, and a dedicated exhibition on the Bang Rachan heroic resistance and its place in Thai national memory. The museum is small, quietly visited, and provides essential historical context for understanding the province's significance — particularly the relationship between Bang Rachan, the fall of Ayutthaya, and Thai national identity. Free or nominal admission; signposted from the town centre.

sources.sourceName: Tourism Authority of Thailand — Sing Buri Province

localFood: Khanom Tan (sugar-palm sweets from Inburi) — small, dense, amber-coloured confections made from the sap of the sugar palm (Borassus flabellifer), stirred slowly with coconut cream over low heat and poured into banana-leaf cups to set; Inburi's most iconic product and among the finest examples of this ancient Central Plains sweet in Thailand

Khanom Mor Gaeng (egg-yolk custard tarts) — silky, barely-set egg-yolk and coconut-milk tarts baked in small clay pots, caramelised on top and perfumed with pandan and shallot oil; a royal Thai dessert still made by hand in Inburi's artisan sweet shops

Pla Chon Tom Som (sour-braise snakehead fish) — freshwater snakehead fish from the province's irrigation canals stewed in a pungent tamarind-based broth with turmeric, lemongrass, and galangal; a quintessential Central Plains farmhouse dish with a sharp, sour, warming character

Khao Tom Moo (rice porridge with pork) — silky slow-cooked jasmine rice porridge enriched with pork bone stock, topped with minced pork, soft-boiled egg, crispy garlic, and pickled vegetables; the standard Sing Buri morning market breakfast, eaten at the municipal market and roadside stalls from 6 AM

Nam Tan Pip Kratong (sugar-palm sap drinks and sweets) — fresh sugar-palm sap harvested from the province's tall palm groves is served chilled as a mildly fermented refreshing drink or reduced to syrup for use in traditional khanom; seasonally available at Inburi and district markets

Mango sticky rice with local Nam Dok Mai mangoes — the flat alluvial plains of Sing Buri and its neighbours produce premium Nam Dok Mai mangoes; paired with warm glutinous rice and thick coconut cream, this classic Central Plains dessert is sold fresh at the main market from March through June

festivals.name: Bang Rachan Heroes Commemoration
festivals.description: An annual provincial ceremony honouring the memory of the Bang Rachan village defenders, typically held in February to align with the Thai historical calendar of the events. The commemoration includes military honours, traditional Thai cultural performances, merit-making ceremonies at Wat Khao Din (associated with the final village battle site), and a fair with food, craft, and history exhibitions in the grounds of the Bang Rachan monument. Thai school groups, veterans' organisations, and provincial officials participate in solemn wreath-laying and candle-lighting ceremonies at the heroes' statues. The event is deeply patriotic in character and provides an insight into how this historical episode continues to shape Thai national identity.

festivals.name: Songkran Sing Buri
festivals.description: The Thai New Year water festival is observed throughout the province with traditional ceremonies centred on the major temples — particularly Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan, where the sacred reclining Buddha image is bathed in a ritual lustral water ceremony that draws large crowds of local devotees. Water-splashing festivities fill the main streets of Sing Buri town and district centres. The festival retains its traditional religious and family character, with merit-making processions, elder blessing ceremonies, and community food-sharing alongside the celebratory water play.

festivals.name: Loy Krathong on the Chao Phraya River
festivals.description: The festival of lights is celebrated along the Chao Phraya River as it flows past the provincial capital, with krathong — decorated floats of banana-leaf, flowers, and candles — released onto the water to carry away misfortune and pay homage to the river spirit. The flat terrain and extensive canal network of Sing Buri make the flickering candlelight particularly beautiful as it reflects across the water. Temple fairs, traditional music, and communal merit-making activities mark the full-moon evening throughout the province.

culture: Sing Buri's cultural identity is rooted in two interlocking values: historical sacrifice and Buddhist devotion. The Bang Rachan story — ordinary villagers choosing to fight and die rather than abandon their community and their faith — has made the province a touchstone of Thai national memory and a place of pilgrimage for those seeking to reconnect with the values of courage, loyalty, and self-sacrifice. School curricula across Thailand teach the Bang Rachan story; films and television dramas have retold it for successive generations; and the monument complex in Bang Rachan district receives a steady stream of visitors for whom the site carries genuine emotional weight. This historical consciousness coexists with a deep vein of Buddhist devotion, expressed most powerfully at Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan, whose reclining Buddha is approached with the reverence given to an image of particular spiritual potency. The act of offering gold leaf to the Buddha's face, of pressing one's palms together before the serene reclining figure, connects Sing Buri's residents to centuries of Central Plains Buddhist practice. The Inburi sweet-making tradition adds a gentler, domestic dimension to the province's cultural character — the preservation of artisan khanom recipes as an act of cultural memory, a link to the tastes and techniques of grandmothers and great-grandmothers. In Sing Buri, history, faith, and the rhythms of agricultural life are not separate categories but a single, integrated way of being.
gettingThere: Sing Buri city is approximately 142 km north of Bangkok via Highway 32 (the Asia Highway through the Central Plains). The most practical route from Bangkok is by bus or minivan from the Northern Bus Terminal (Mo Chit); buses to Sing Buri run several times daily with journey times of approximately 2 to 2.5 hours and fares around 100–130 THB. There is a railway line passing through the province — the northern main line from Bangkok Hua Lamphong or Bang Sue stops at Sing Buri station — though train frequency is limited compared to the bus service. From Ayutthaya the drive north on Highway 32 takes approximately 1 hour (75 km). From Ang Thong to the south, Sing Buri city is approximately 40 km north by road. From Lop Buri to the east, the provincial capital is approximately 50 km west. Driving is the most flexible option for exploring the province's dispersed attractions, including Bang Rachan in the west and Inburi district in the north.
gettingAround: Sing Buri city is small and walkable around the market and riverside area, but the key attractions are spread across the province's six districts. The Bang Rachan monument and village site are approximately 20 km west of the provincial capital; Inburi district is approximately 25 km to the north; Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan is located in the capital district. Renting a motorcycle (150–250 THB/day) or a car (from Ayutthaya or Bangkok rental agencies) is the most practical approach for a full provincial circuit. Songthaew (shared pickup truck taxis) run between the capital and major district towns including Inburi and Bang Rachan, but services are infrequent and schedules irregular — confirm locally before relying on them. Tuk-tuks and motorcycle taxis handle short trips within the capital. A chartered songthaew or taxi for a half-day circuit covering Bang Rachan, Wat Phra Non, and Inburi costs approximately 600–1,000 THB and can be arranged at the main market. The province's completely flat terrain makes cycling a genuinely pleasant option during the cool season.
travelTips: Combine Sing Buri with Ang Thong (40 km south) and Ayutthaya (115 km south) or Lop Buri (50 km east) for a multi-province Central Plains loop from Bangkok — Highway 32 connects all three efficiently
The Bang Rachan monument is most evocative visited in the quiet of early morning or late afternoon when tour groups are absent; a Thai-language audio guide is available at the museum
Dress modestly at all temples including Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan — shoulders and knees must be covered; sarongs are available for loan at the entrance
The Inburi sweet market operates from early morning and is most vibrant before noon — buy khanom tan and khanom mor gaeng freshly made to take back as gifts (of-wan)
Accommodation options in Sing Buri city are limited and basic — most international visitors treat the province as a day trip from Bangkok, Ayutthaya, or Lop Buri
The province's flat terrain makes cycling an excellent option in the November–February cool season; bicycles can sometimes be borrowed from guesthouses or rented from market stalls

The rice-paddy landscape is at its most photogenic during the planting season (June–July, deep green) and harvest (November–December, golden) — early morning light across the flat plains is especially beautiful

cities.name: Sing Buri
cities.description: The compact provincial capital on the Chao Phraya River, containing the main market, Wat Phra Non Chakkrasi Worawihan, the provincial museum, and the central transport hub for reaching the province's outlying attractions.

cities.name: Bang Rachan
cities.description: The historically significant district approximately 20 km west of the provincial capital, home to the Bang Rachan Heroes Monument and associated village sites commemorating the 1765 resistance against the Burmese army.

cities.name: Inburi
cities.description: A northern district approximately 25 km from the capital, nationally renowned as the centre of traditional Thai sweet-making (khanom); artisan confectioners produce sugar-palm and coconut-based sweets at the Inburi market and in surrounding village workshops.
